title: CC Exercises — Composable Storefront (Spartacus)
description: Hands-on exercises covering Spartacus setup, CMS component mapping, custom components, service overrides, layout configuration, and B2B features.

sections.title: Exercise 1 — Spartacus Project Setup & CMS Exploration
sections.content:
Objective

Set up a Spartacus project from scratch, connect it to a Commerce Cloud OCC backend, and explore how CMS-driven page composition works.

Prerequisites

Node.js (v16+), Angular CLI, and access to a Commerce Cloud OCC endpoint (can be a shared dev instance or the SAP Spartacus demo backend).

Steps

  1. Generate the project: Use the Angular CLI to create a new project, then add Spartacus via schematics:
    ng new my-storefront --style=scss --routing=true
cd my-storefront
ng add @spartacus/schematics
    Follow the prompts to configure the base URL, base site, and features to install.
  2. Configure OCC: In your app module (or feature config), verify the OCC base URL points to your backend:
    provideConfig({
  backend: { occ: { baseUrl: 'https://your-occ-backend.com' } }
})
  3. Run the storefront: ng serve. Navigate to http://localhost:4200. Verify the homepage loads with CMS-driven content.
  4. Inspect CMS data: Open the browser DevTools → Network tab. Filter by /cms/. Observe the CMS API calls: the page request, the slot/component data returned. Map what you see in the browser to the CMS structure: identify which slots and components render which visual elements.
  5. Test a product page: Navigate to a product. Observe the OCC product API call in DevTools. Note the fields parameter Spartacus sends. Identify the CMS ProductPage structure (which slots exist, which components each contains).

Validation

The storefront renders correctly with CMS-driven content. You can trace every visual element on the page back to a CMS component type in the API response. Document at least 5 component type → Angular component mappings you observe (e.g. BannerComponent → cx-banner).

sections.title: Exercise 2 — Custom CMS Component in Spartacus
sections.content:
Objective

Create a custom Angular component that renders the FeatureHighlightComponent CMS type you created in the Tier 4 CMS exercises, and map it in Spartacus.

Prerequisites

The FeatureHighlightComponent CMS type must exist in the Commerce Cloud backend (from cc-exercises-tier4, Exercise 2). If not, create it via ImpEx with attributes: icon, title, description, linkUrl.

Steps

  1. Generate the Angular component:
    ng generate component components/feature-highlight
  2. Implement the component: Inject CmsComponentData<any> and use the async pipe to render the CMS data:
    @Component({
  selector: 'app-feature-highlight',
  template: `
    <div class="feature-highlight" *ngIf="data$ | async as data">
      <span class="icon">{{ data.icon }}</span>
      <h3>{{ data.title }}</h3>
      <p>{{ data.description }}</p>
      <a *ngIf="data.linkUrl" [href]="data.linkUrl">Learn more</a>
    </div>
  `
})
export class FeatureHighlightComponent {
  data$ = inject(CmsComponentData).data$;
}
  3. Register the CMS mapping: In your app module or a feature module:
    provideConfig({
  cmsComponents: {
    FeatureHighlightComponent: {
      component: FeatureHighlightComponent
    }
  }
})
  4. Style the component: Add SCSS styling to match your brand — icon in a coloured circle, bold title, muted description text, link with hover effect.
  5. Test: Navigate to the page where you placed FeatureHighlightComponent instances (the /training page from Tier 4 exercises). Verify they render with the correct data from CMS.

Bonus Challenge

Make the component responsive: show as a horizontal card on desktop and a vertical stack on mobile. Use Spartacus's responsive breakpoint variables in your SCSS.

Validation

The custom CMS component renders correctly on the page. CMS data (icon, title, description, link) flows from the backend through OCC to the Angular component. Changes made in Backoffice or SmartEdit (e.g. changing the title) appear on the storefront after sync — no code deployment needed.

sections.title: Exercise 3 — Override a Standard Component & Service
sections.content:
Objective

Replace a standard Spartacus component with a custom version, and override a service to add custom business logic — demonstrating both presentation and logic customisation.

Steps

Part A — Component Override

  1. Choose a standard component to override — for example, the MiniCartComponent (the cart icon/count in the header).
  2. Generate a custom component: ng generate component components/custom-mini-cart.
  3. Implement it to show not just the item count but also the cart total and a loyalty points preview (e.g. "You'll earn X points with this purchase"). Inject ActiveCartFacade to get cart data.
  4. Map it via provideConfig to replace the standard MiniCartComponent.
  5. Verify: add items to cart and confirm your custom mini-cart shows the count, total, and points estimate.

Part B — Service Override

  1. Create a CustomProductService extending Spartacus's ProductService.
  2. Override the get() method: call super.get(), then pipe() the result to add a computed property — for example, a priceCategory field that is "Budget" (under €100), "Mid-Range" (€100-500), or "Premium" (over €500).
  3. Provide your custom service in place of the standard one:
    { provide: ProductService, useClass: CustomProductService }
  4. Modify the product detail component (via component override or template override) to display the priceCategory badge.

Validation

The mini-cart shows custom data alongside the standard count. The product detail page shows the price category badge. Both customisations work without modifying any Spartacus library code. Removing the provideConfig entries and providers restores the standard behaviour.

sections.title: Exercise 4 — B2B Organisation & Checkout Exploration
sections.content:
Objective

Explore Spartacus B2B features: set up an organisation structure on the backend, then walk through the B2B checkout flow and approval process in the storefront.

Prerequisites

A Commerce Cloud backend with B2B extensions enabled (b2bcommerce, b2bapprovalprocess). Spartacus with @spartacus/organization and @spartacus/checkout/b2b installed.

Steps

Part A — Organisation Setup (Backend via ImpEx)

  1. Create a B2B organisation structure via ImpEx:
    • Root unit: TrainingCorp
    • Child units: TrainingCorp_Engineering, TrainingCorp_Marketing
    • B2B customers: a Buyer in Engineering, an Approver in Engineering, and an Administrator at TrainingCorp root level.
    • A Budget: €10,000 for Q2 2026, assigned to TrainingCorp.
    • A Cost Centre: CC_Engineering linked to the Engineering unit and the budget.
    • Permissions: Order Threshold of €2,000 for the Buyer (orders above this need approval).

Part B — My Company in Spartacus

  1. Log in as the Administrator user on the Spartacus storefront.
  2. Navigate to My Company (Organisation management area).
  3. Explore: verify the unit hierarchy is displayed correctly (TrainingCorp → Engineering, Marketing). View users, budgets, and cost centres.
  4. Try creating a new user from the Spartacus UI — assign them to the Marketing unit with the Buyer role.

Part C — B2B Checkout & Approval

  1. Log in as the Engineering Buyer.
  2. Add products to the cart totalling more than €2,000 (above the permission threshold).
  3. Proceed to checkout: select Account Payment, enter a PO Number, select the Engineering cost centre, choose a delivery address (restricted to approved addresses), and submit.
  4. The order should enter Pending Approval (not place directly) because the threshold is exceeded.
  5. Log in as the Engineering Approver. Navigate to Order Approval. Find the pending order, review its details (products, total, cost centre, PO number), and approve it.
  6. Log back in as the Buyer and verify the order status changed to approved/placed.

Bonus Challenge

Repeat with an order under €2,000. Verify it places directly without entering the approval workflow. This confirms the permission threshold is working correctly.

Validation

The full B2B flow works end-to-end: organisation self-service in My Company, B2B checkout with cost centre and PO number, automatic approval routing when the threshold is exceeded, and approver review/approval. Document each step with screenshots if possible — this walkthrough demonstrates the complete B2B commerce capability.
